channel.title: +Non Profits on Vishvas's notes
channel.description: Recent content in +Non Profits on Vishvas's notes

item.title: 80G exemption
item.description:
Common Reasons for 80G Refusal

For Vedapatasalas/Religious Trusts

For a trust engaged in Vedic education or temple-associated activities (Religious cum Charitable), the Income Tax Department frequently raises the following objections to reject 80G applications:

  • The “Wholly Religious” Classification: Under Indian tax law, purely religious trusts are not eligible for 80G registration. Only “charitable” or “charitable-cum-religious” trusts are eligible. If the Commissioner of Income Tax (Exemptions) [CIT(E)] views the teaching of Vedas and temple kainkaryam as purely religious rituals rather than educational or cultural preservation, they may reject the 80G application.
  • Exceeding the 5% Religious Expenditure Limit (Section 80G(5B)): By law, a composite (charitable-cum-religious) trust can spend money on religious activities, but this expenditure must not exceed 5% of its total income in that financial year. If the trust’s accounts show that a major portion of funds is spent on temple recitations, rituals, or daily worship, the CIT(E) will reject the 80G on the grounds of violating this 5% limit.
  • Benefiting a Specific Caste or Community: Section 80G(5)(iii) states that the trust’s activities must not be restricted to any particular religious community or caste. If the CIT(E) interprets the Vedapatasala as serving or admitting only a specific community, it can trigger a rejection.
  • Technical or Procedural Rejections: Under the updated rules, if a trust files the wrong form (e.g., choosing the wrong clause in Form 10A/10AB) or fails to submit audited accounts and registration deeds on time, the application is rejected on hyper-technical grounds.

Can an 80G Refusal Be Appealed?

Yes, absolutely. If the CIT(Exemptions) rejects an 80G application, the trust has a clear legal remedy to appeal the decision.

item.title: PKV Trust
item.description:
• Name of the Trust: Perumal Koil Vedapatasala Trust
  • Address: New No. 7, Old No. 4, 1st Floor, Thiruvengadam Street Extension, R K Nagar, Mandaveli, Chennai – 600028, Tamil Nadu.
  • PAN of the Trust: AAATP0787H
  • Unique Registration Number (URN): AAATP0787H25CH01
  • Section under which registered: Section 12AB(1)(b) (for religious cum charitable activities).
  • Date of Order: February 21, 2026.
  • Validity Period: Assessment Years 2027-28 to 2036-37 (a 10-year block of approval).
  • Approving Authority: Premanand Jothy, Commissioner of Income Tax (Exemption), Chennai.

Form 10AD

Benefits

  • Tax Exemption on Trust Income: This registration is the foundation of tax exemption for a non-profit in India. It means that the income the trust receives (including voluntary contributions and donations) will not be taxed, provided the trust utilizes the funds for its stated religious and charitable purposes.

Key Conditions the Trust Must Follow (Pages 3 & 4)

To maintain this tax-exempt status over the 10-year period, the trust must adhere to several rules listed in the Annexure:

item.title: Registration
item.description:
Historically, trusts were registered under Section 12AA. The government later updated the law, requiring all trusts to migrate to or register under Section 12AB.

It is Form No. 10AD, which is the formal order granting registration to a trust under Section 12AB of the Income Tax Act, 1961. This document confirms that the trust has successfully secured this crucial registration.
